verses.text: The children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD; and the LORD delivered them into the hand of the Philistines forty years. Guidance: Forty years — the book's longest oppression. And the chapter's quietest detail: Israel does not cry out this time. After the Ammonite war they accommodated themselves to the Philistine yoke. The cycle has flattened: sin, oppression, no repentance. God will raise a deliverer anyway, but Israel does not even ask for him. Samson will come unrequested into a people too compromised to call for him.

There was a certain man of Zorah, of the family of the Danites, whose name was Manoah; and his wife was barren, and bare not. The angel of the LORD appeared unto the woman, and said unto her, Behold now, thou art barren, and bearest not: but thou shalt conceive, and bear a son. Now therefore beware, I pray thee, and drink not wine nor strong drink, and eat not any unclean thing: for, lo, thou shalt conceive, and bear a son; and no razor shall come on his head: for the child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb: and he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines. Guidance: The angel finds the wife first — she is not named in the chapter (a striking absence in a story about a son), only by what she is and isn't. Barren. The same pattern as Sarah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth, Mary: God starts the deliverer in a womb that medicine had pronounced shut. The Nazirite vow (Num 6) — no grapes, no shaving, no corpse contact — is normally a temporary adult choice. For Samson it is a lifetime starting in utero. Even his mother is to keep it during pregnancy. The boy is set apart before he has a chance to choose. The phrase 'he shall begin to deliver' is also striking — Samson will only begin the job. David will finish it. The most spectacular biography is sometimes only the opening chapter of a longer story.

Then the woman came and told her husband, saying, A man of God came unto me, and his countenance was like the countenance of an angel of God, very terrible: but I asked him not whence he was, neither told he me his name: but he said unto me, Behold, thou shalt conceive, and bear a son; and now drink no wine nor strong drink, neither eat any unclean thing: for the child shall be a Nazarite to God from the womb to the day of his death. Guidance: Her report is careful. She does not exaggerate the visitor's identity ('a man of God'); she names her own gap ('I asked him not whence he was'); she repeats the instructions accurately. Three signs of a faithful witness: caution about claims, honesty about what you did not learn, accuracy about what you did. Even the Spirit's most extraordinary messengers can be reported in plain, modest sentences.

Then the woman came and told her husband, saying, A man of God came unto me, and his countenance was like the countenance of an angel of God, very terrible: but I asked him not whence he was, neither told he me his name: but he said unto me, Behold, thou shalt conceive, and bear a son; and now drink no wine nor strong drink, neither eat any unclean thing: for the child shall be a Nazarite to God from the womb to the day of his death. Guidance: Her report is careful. She does not exaggerate the visitor's identity ('a man of God'); she names her own gap ('I asked him not whence he was'); she repeats the instructions accurately. Three signs of a faithful witness: caution about claims, honesty about what you did not learn, accuracy about what you did. Even the Spirit's most extraordinary messengers can be reported in plain, modest sentences.

Then Manoah intreated the LORD, and said, O my Lord, let the man of God which thou didst send come again unto us, and teach us what we shall do unto the child that shall be born. God hearkened to the voice of Manoah; and the angel of God came again unto the woman as she sat in the field: but Manoah her husband was not with her. The woman made haste, and ran, and shewed her husband, and said unto him, Behold, the man hath appeared unto me, that came unto me the other day. Guidance: Manoah's prayer is humble — not 'come back so I can verify' but 'teach us what to do.' God answers prayers asked in willingness to learn, but the answer comes to his wife again, not him. She runs to fetch him; the husband has to be brought to where God already is. Sometimes faithful spouses encounter God before the spiritual head of the home does — and the order of revelation is God's choice, not the family's hierarchy.

Manoah arose, and went after his wife, and came to the man, and said unto him, Art thou the man that spakest unto the woman? And he said, I am. Manoah said, Now let thy words come to pass. How shall we order the child, and how shall we do unto him? The angel of the LORD said unto Manoah, Of all that I said unto the woman let her beware. She may not eat of any thing that cometh of the vine, neither let her drink wine or strong drink, nor eat any unclean thing: all that I commanded her let her observe. Guidance: Manoah asks for instructions on the child; the angel pointedly answers about the mother. The vow's first practitioner has to be her. Notice the gentle redirection — the father wants a parenting manual; the angel says the holy life begins in the womb because of the mother's diet. The spiritual formation of children starts in choices made before they are born. The angel's repetition is not an insult to Manoah; it is a clarification that the message was complete the first time.

Manoah said unto the angel of the LORD, I pray thee, let us detain thee, until we shall have made ready a kid for thee. The angel of the LORD said unto Manoah, Though thou detain me, I will not eat of thy bread: and if thou wilt offer a burnt offering, thou must offer it unto the LORD. For Manoah knew not that he was an angel of the LORD. Manoah said unto the angel of the LORD, What is thy name, that when thy sayings come to pass we may do thee honour? And the angel of the LORD said unto him, Why askest thou thus after my name, seeing it is secret? Guidance: Manoah offers hospitality. The angel redirects again: feed the LORD, not me. Then Manoah asks for the name. The angel replies with a Hebrew word the KJV translates 'secret' but is more literally 'wonderful' — the same word Isaiah 9:6 uses of the coming Messiah: 'his name shall be called Wonderful.' Manoah is conversing with someone whose name is itself a prophecy. He cannot mark this messenger with honor; the messenger is too high to be filed under any honor he can give. Some encounters with God leave us unable to even compliment Him properly.

So Manoah took a kid with a meat offering, and offered it upon a rock unto the LORD: and the angel did wondrously; and Manoah and his wife looked on. For it came to pass, when the flame went up toward heaven from off the altar, that the angel of the LORD ascended in the flame of the altar. And Manoah and his wife looked on it, and fell on their faces to the ground. Guidance: Same pattern as Gideon (6:21) — fire rises from the rock, the angel disappears. But here the angel rides the flame up. The visitor enters the flame and takes it home. Manoah and his wife do the only thing left to do: face down. Worship is the body's first language when the mind has finally caught up to what it has been seeing.

The angel of the LORD did no more appear to Manoah and to his wife. Then Manoah knew that he was an angel of the LORD. Manoah said unto his wife, We shall surely die, because we have seen God. But his wife said unto him, If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands, neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor would as at this time have told us such things as these. Guidance: Manoah panics — like Gideon (6:22), he assumes seeing God means death. His wife's reply is the chapter's quiet theological masterpiece. Her logic is irrefutable: if God wanted us dead, He would not have accepted our offering, shown us this, or made us promises about a future son. Faith reasons forward from God's evidence rather than backward from human fear. The husband's fear is honest; the wife's faith is sharper. Sometimes the mother of the deliverer is the firmer believer in the household before the deliverer is even born.

The woman bare a son, and called his name Samson: and the child grew, and the LORD blessed him. The Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol. Guidance: 'Samson' (Shimshon) — from the Hebrew for 'sun' — a fitting name for a boy whose mother first met heaven in a flame. He grows, he is blessed, and the Spirit of the LORD begins to 'move' him (literally 'impel' or 'trouble') in the camp of Dan. The verb is unusual; the Spirit pulses through him at intervals, not constantly. This is the foreshadowing of his career: gifted but not steady. The Spirit will come on him in bursts. The blessed boy growing up between Zorah and Eshtaol will be a man of moments — extraordinary on his best days, disastrous on his worst. The chapter that began with a quiet barren woman closes with the boy who will bring the chapter-after-chapter wrestling match between divine gift and human folly.
